name: Evening primrose oil
oneliner: GLA precursor. Mixed eczema data; safer than spectacular.
eyebrow: Supplement · Barrier & inflammation · Evening primrose oil
headline: Evening primrose
dek: Evening primrose oil delivers gamma-linolenic acid (GLA), a precursor to prostaglandins involved in skin barrier function. The eczema literature is mixed: positive in early small trials, less convincing in the larger Cochrane reviews. A safer-than-spectacular molecule with a defensible role in mild atopic skin.
bottom: A modest, safe, defensible adjunct for mild atopic skin; not a primary treatment. The Cochrane verdict is honest; the case for trying it is reasonable.

evidence.note: The 2013 Cochrane review concluded oral evening primrose oil does not meaningfully improve eczema vs placebo. Earlier small trials were more positive.
Several small positive trials; methodologically weaker. Reasonable trial in mild cases.
Better-supported off-label use than the eczema indication.
Inconsistent. Not a primary recommendation for menopausal symptoms.

forms.note: Standard low-dose. Two capsules twice daily for atopic skin trial.
Higher per-capsule dose. Useful for hitting the trial protocol with fewer capsules.
Cheaper alternative source of GLA. Equivalent endpoints in head-to-head trials.
Vegetarian / vegan-friendly GLA source. Less-studied than EPO but mechanistically equivalent.

faq.q: Why is the Cochrane verdict so flat?
faq.a: The pooled analysis weighted more recent, larger, methodologically tighter trials, which generally showed null or marginal effects. The earlier positive trials had smaller sample sizes and less rigorous methodology. Both data sets exist; we present both honestly.

Reasonable in mild atopic skin where the topical-only protocol has plateaued. 12 weeks at the published dose. If no measurable effect, the supplement is not your bottleneck — pivot to topical optimisation, allergen audit, or systemic options if appropriate.
Yes. The two work via partly different prostaglandin pathways; combination is reasonable. Most atopic protocols that include EPO also include 1–2 g EPA+DHA omega-3.

Generally very well-tolerated. Mild GI upset is the most common. Theoretical bleeding-risk concern at high doses with anticoagulants; clinically rarely an issue. Stop one week before elective surgery as a precaution.
